title: Cross-Site Request Forgery (CSRF)
description: Trick a victim's browser into executing unwanted actions on a trusted site where they are currently authenticated.

lesson.summary: Cross-Site Request Forgery (CSRF) is an attack that forces an end user to execute unwanted actions on a web application in which they're currently authenticated. CSRF attacks specifically target state-changing requests, not theft of data.
realWorld: Netflix Account Takeover (Attacker changes email without password prompt)
Bank Transfer Forgery (Auto-submitting hidden form transferring funds)
Router DNS Hijacking (Changing router settings via admin's browser)
mistakes: Using GET requests for state-changing actions
Relying only on CORS (CORS does not prevent CSRF)
Assuming APIs without cookies are immune (Basic Auth is also vulnerable)
tips: Always use SameSite=Lax for session cookies.
Implement double-submit cookies for stateless APIs.
Use modern frameworks that provide CSRF protection out of the box.

arena.attackDescription: An attacker has hosted a malicious page that auto-submits a POST request to our `/api/user/email` endpoint. Admin is currently logged in.
